result: The first light of morning doesn’t arrive; it leaks, a pale bruise spreading between blinds and the wall. Out in the alley a single magpie lands on a trash-can lid, pivoting its head as if counting unreturned favors. I think of my grandmother who taught me to salute magpies, and of how she talked to houseplants in nine languages, swearing each tongue coaxed a different bloom. Nothing of hers ever died, not even the Christmas cactus that finally surrendered after the hospital called. Today my own plants fake photosynthesis; they know an impostor hunches at the window watering them with tap water and apologies. The kettle clicks, referee to the resonant silence. Coffee drips in dilatory steps, a performance art too slow to matter but impossible to abandon. Yesterday’s emails wait like pets who have learned to open doors. Their needs are sincere and endless: Yes, I did receive the proposal; no, I am not the person who signs checks. Still, I imagine a parallel inbox where every subject line reads You are loved come home. I would click them all, let them soft-reboot my pulse. Outside, traffic choreographs a choreography that never revises, and I practice breathing on beat, inhaling four, exhaling a whispered allegiance to whatever second chances the asphalt still hides. The magpie leaves, taking its unfinished algebra with it. The lid stays raised in salute or surrender. I decide tomorrow belongs long before I do and go to greet it with lukewarm coffee and an open palm, ready to bargain.
